metadata.summary: Define, measure, and improve activation rate by correlating candidate actions with D30 retention, benchmark ranges by product type, and optimize your onboarding funnel.

content.title: Activation Metrics
content.summary: A methodology for defining, measuring, and improving the activation rate — the percentage of signups who reach their first value moment (the "Aha moment"). Covers how to identify the right activation event by correlating candidate actions with D30 retention, how to measure time-bounded activation rates, benchmark ranges by product type, and a worked optimization funnel template.

why.p: Improving activation is often the highest-ROI growth lever because the same acquisition spend produces 3-5x more converted customers. The activation event must correlate with retention, not just signal setup completion — wrong activation targets (e.g., "created profile" instead of "completed first task") optimize for vanity actions that don't predict revenue.

The multiplier effect: with 20% activation rate, 100 sign-ups produce 4 retained D30 users and 1 converted customer (1% signup-to-paid). With 60% activation rate, the same 100 sign-ups produce 18 retained D30 users and 5 converted customers (5% signup-to-paid). Same acquisition cost, 5x revenue. Activation improvement compounds through every downstream funnel stage.

list.item: Users sign up but don't return after the first session.
Onboarding completion is high but D30 retention is low — possible activation misdefinition.
Pre-scaling acquisition: fix activation before spending more on top-of-funnel.
Redesigning onboarding flow and need a metric to validate the change.
Pre-PMF: if core value is undefined, activation measurement is premature.
Enterprise SaaS with multi-month sales cycles — activation happens over weeks, not sessions.
Products with no analytics infrastructure — defining the metric without tracking it produces no signal.

section.name: Defining the Activation Event

rule.statement: Define activation as the specific action that most strongly correlates with D30 retention — not setup completion, not first login, not profile creation.
rule.rationale: Setup actions (profile creation, email verification) are necessary but not sufficient for value delivery. The activation event must mark the moment the user experienced the core value proposition. Only retention correlation validates this.

section.p: Activation is the moment when a new user first experiences the product's core value — the "Aha moment." Flow: signup → onboarding (setup done) → activation (first real value received) → engagement → conversion.

rule.statement: Run a four-step retention correlation process: list candidate actions, measure D30 retention for users who did each action versus those who did not, rank by retention lift, and validate that the top action is achievable in the first session.
rule.rationale: Without empirical correlation testing, teams optimize for the most visible user action rather than the most predictive one. The correlation test eliminates guesswork and frequently reveals surprising results (e.g., "invited team member" predicting retention better than "completed a task").

section.p: Good vs bad activation definitions by product type: SaaS tool — "completed core workflow" (good) vs "used features" (bad); marketplace — "made first transaction" vs "browsed listings"; social — "connected with a friend" vs "created profile"; e-commerce — "made first purchase" vs "added to cart"; mobile game — "completed level 3" vs "downloaded app".

section.name: Measuring Activation Rate and Benchmarks

rule.statement: Always measure activation in time-bounded windows (Day 0, Day 7, Day 30) rather than as a cumulative all-time rate; time-bounded rates reveal where in the onboarding window drop-off occurs.
rule.rationale: Cumulative activation rates grow artificially over time as late activators contribute. Time-bounded windows provide stable, comparable cohort metrics that inform specific onboarding interventions.

section.p: Formulas: Basic activation rate = users who activated / total sign-ups × 100%. Day 0 activation = activated same day / sign-ups that day. Day 7 activation = activated within 7 days / sign-ups 7+ days ago. Day 30 activation = activated within 30 days / sign-ups 30+ days ago.

Most activation happens in the first 3 days. Curve shape: rapid rise in Days 0-3, slower accumulation Days 3-7, plateau after Day 14. Interventions before Day 3 have the highest multiplier. If Day 30 cumulative is only 5% higher than Day 7, onboarding fixes should target the first session, not re-engagement email.
B2B SaaS: good 30-50%, great 50-70%. Consumer app: good 40-60%, great 60-80%. E-commerce: good 20-40%, great 40-60%. Mobile game: good 50-70%, great 70-90%. Marketplace: good 15-30%, great 30-50%.
By activation timeframe: same session (simple products) 40-60%; Day 0 standard target 50-70%; Day 7 for complex products 60-80%; Day 30 for enterprise SaaS 70-90%.

example.title: Slack
example.p: Activation metric: sent first message in team channel. Optimization journey V1 to V5: V1 35% (basic signup) → V2 45% (team creation prompt) → V3 55% (pre-filled #general channel) → V4 65% (suggested first message) → V5 75% (Slackbot tutorial). Key insight: each 10pp gain came from reducing the friction between account creation and the first social action.

example.title: Dropbox
example.p: Activation metric: uploaded first file. Key optimizations: desktop app prompt (+20%), showing sync folder after install (+15%), drag-and-drop tutorial (+10%), gamified checklist (+25%). The gamified checklist — install desktop app, upload first file, install mobile, share a folder, invite a friend — produced the largest single activation lift.

example.title: Duolingo
example.p: Activation metric: completed first lesson. TTV: under 2 minutes. Key insight: first lesson delivered before account creation — signup happened after value was already experienced. Result: 90%+ Day 0 activation rate. Gamification (XP, streak, progress bar) reinforced the value signal immediately.

example.title: Canva
example.p: Activation metric: created and downloaded first design. Key optimizations: templates first (+40%), social media size presets (+15%), one-click download (+10%), suggested search (+20%). Empty state → template gallery transformation was the single highest-impact change — it eliminated the blank-canvas paralysis that blocked activation.

rule.statement: Reduce the number of decisions a new user must make before the first value moment; every choice is a potential exit point.
rule.rationale: Each of the four examples above reduced friction by providing a pre-made starting point (suggested message, template, pre-filled channel) rather than asking users to create from scratch. The activation event happened sooner because the path to it was shorter.

template.purpose: Activation funnel analysis template with definition, drop-off analysis, and optimization plan
